title: Check yourself
questions.0.q: Your <h1> comes out large. A friend opens the same page in a different browser and the heading is a slightly different size. Who got it wrong?
questions.0.options.0: You — you should have set the size
questions.0.options.1: Nobody — you only said it was a heading; the size is the browser's call
questions.0.options.2: Your friend — his browser is old
questions.0.options.3: The browser — it should show the same thing everywhere
questions.0.hint: Go back and read what you actually wrote. Is there a number in it? Is there a size at all?
questions.0.explain: You wrote <h1>. That says "top-level heading" and nothing else — there is no size anywhere in it. The size came from the browser's built-in styles. Those are not the same everywhere, and they change over time. You got nothing wrong. You just haven't reached the part where you get to say how it looks.
questions.1.q: You forget the closing tag: you write <h1>Ivan and nothing more. What happens?
questions.1.options.0: The browser shows an error and stops
questions.1.options.1: Nothing — the closing tag is optional
questions.1.options.2: The browser guesses where the heading ends, and usually guesses wrong
questions.1.hint: The browser is not a teacher. It doesn't stop, doesn't correct you, doesn't tell you where you slipped. Think about what it does instead.
questions.1.explain: The browser almost never stops with an error — it tries to clean up the mess for you. The trouble is that its idea of "cleaned up" rarely matches yours.
questions.2.q: A friend wants his sentence to be blue. What do you tell him?
questions.2.options.0: There is a tag for blue — he just needs to find it
questions.2.options.1: HTML does not deal with colours; that is CSS's job
questions.2.options.2:
He should replace

with something more colourful
questions.2.options.3: The colour is set inside the text itself
questions.2.hint: Ask yourself what HTML describes. What a thing IS — or how it looks?
questions.2.explain: HTML says what a thing is, not how it looks. If you are hunting for a "blue tag", you still think you are drawing. But you are describing.
questions.3.q: You press Enter three times in the code to separate two sentences. What does the browser see?
questions.3.options.0: Three blank lines — exactly as you wrote them
questions.3.options.2: Nothing — blank lines in the code do not exist for it
questions.3.options.3: An error, because blank lines confuse the page
questions.3.hint: The browser reads tags. Enter is not a tag. So what does it do with it?
questions.3.explain:
Blank lines in code are for your eyes, not the browser's. If you want separation, you must say so with a tag — for example

. Otherwise everything glues into one line.
